data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, Dow futures rise after choppy start to 2025
data.description:
Stock index futures were higher on Friday, a day after Wall Street kicked off 2025 with a choppy first trading session.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) +0.4%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) +0.6% and Dow futures (INDU) +0.2%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 1 basis point to 4.56%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 1 basis point to 4.25%.

U.S. stocks on Thursday experienced a choppy first trading session of 2025, in which they initially jumped, then seesawed and finally ended in negative territory. Wall Street is now on a five-day losing streak.

"The first trading day has been a very poor guide to the rest of the year in recent times, so we shouldn’t extrapolate things too far," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

For U.S. equities there was a much more negative story, with the S&P 500 posting a fifth consecutive loss, driven by even deeper losses for the Magnificent 7, Allen added.

On the economic side, the December ISM Manufacturing Index is forecasted to land during market hours. The economists on an average expect it to come in at 48.2.

"A U.S. business sentiment survey is due. Partisan bias gives upside risks," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

There are a couple of Federal Reserve speakers also lined up for today, including Richmond Fed President, Thomas Barkin and San Francisco Fed President, Mary Daly.

data.title: Apple's China woes linger, foreign phone sales slump in November 2024 - report
data.description:
Shipments of Foreign-branded phones, including Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) iPhone, to China tumbled by 47.4% in November 2024 compared to a year ago, Reuters reported, citing data from a government-affiliated research firm.

The data from the China Academy of Information and Communications Technology, or CAICT, showed that it was the fourth month of year-over-year decline. For November 2024, the shipments fell to 3.04 million units, versus 5.769 million units in the year ago period, the report added, following calculations based on the data.

Shipments of phones in China, which include domestic brands, declined 5.1% year-over-year in November 2024 to 29.61 million handsets, according to the report.

Data from CAICT for October 2024 had also shown that shipments of foreign branded smartphones had plunged versus the year ago period. Apple is the largest foreign smartphone maker in China.

Apple's iPhones have been facing tough competition from the resurgent Huawei Technologies and from other local smartphone brands in the Chinese market.

On Thursday, the U.S. tech giant announced a promotion in China which would run from Jan. 4 to Jan. 7, offering discounts of up to 500 yuan (about $68) on its latest iPhone models in China.

Huawei had cut the prices of several high-end devices, including smartphones, by up to 3,000 yuan (about $411) over the weekend on Chinese e-commerce platform JD.com's "Super Brand Day" promotion.

In November 2024, Apple saw a double-digit year-on-year decline in iPhone sales at China's two-week Singles' Day shopping festival, as it faced pressure from an "abnormally high number" of competitors, according to Counterpoint Research.

Apple is also facing an uphill task in China of trying to integrate AI features within its products. Meanwhile, Chinese tech giant Huawei has seen a turnaround. The company was once in competition with Apple and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) to be the world's biggest handset maker until U.S. restrictions, starting in 2019, began to curb its access to chip manufacturing tools needed to produce its most advanced models.

But for over a year, the Chinese tech giant has been launching several high-end flagship phones. Last year in November, Huawei unveiled its flagship Mate 70 smartphone models and highlighted its new operating system, HarmonyOS NEXT. Mate 70 is the successor to the Mate 60 series, which Huawei quietly launched in August 2023, surprising many.

In September 2024, Huawei received over 3M pre-orders for its triple-folding smartphone Mate XT on its Vmall website. The company had held an event to launch the phone and other new products just hours after Apple (AAPL) unveiled its latest line of iPhones, the iPhone 16 series, on Sept. 9, 2024.

data.title: Biden to block U.S. Steel's sale to Japan's Nippon - reports
data.description:
Having a 123-year-old iconic American steel giant be taken over by a Japanese producer has not gone well in the US of A. Multiple reports suggest that President Biden will block Nippon Steel's (OTCPK:NPSCY) $14.9B deal for U.S. Steel (NYSE:X) on Friday after interagency committee CFIUS left the decision in his hands. Nippon Steel (OTCPK:NPSCY) will have to fork over a $565M penalty payment to U.S. Steel (X) should the deal collapse, in a move that will have reverberations for M&A even among the closest U.S. allies for the foreseeable future.

Premarket movement: U.S. Steel (X) is down 8% to $30/share (well below the $55/share buyout price agreed to by Nippon).

Backdrop: Deep-pocketed Nippon (OTCPK:NPSCY) hoped the acquisition would expand its global footprint at a time when many Japanese firms are looking for international growth as their country deals with a shrinking population crisis. Recall that the steel industry has also been in focus following the approval of the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, which is expected to boost U.S. steel demand for investment in bridges, railways and other projects due to "Buy American" provisions in the legislation. Nippon (OTCPK:NPSCY) had estimated that the only major speed bump would be union support, while investment concessions would yield the necessary political leverage.

That didn't happen. A number of Senators initially voiced opposition, like Pennsylvania's John Fetterman, who represents one of the most important states involved in the American steel industry. "It's absolutely outrageous," he declared. "Steel is always about security - both our national security and the economic security of our steel communities. I am committed to doing anything I can do, using my platform and my position, to block this foreign." Bipartisan support against the foreign takeover later picked up, and one of the final nails in the coffin happened during the election cycle, when both Biden and Trump tried to shore up support for the blue-collar vote.

What's next? Prior to the agreement from Nippon (OTCPK:NPSCY), there was a bidding war that saw U.S. Steel (X) reject an unsolicited $7.3B cash-and-stock bid from rival Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF). That deal was less than half of what Nippon would pay for the company only four months later. U.S. Steel (X) would go on to announce a strategic review process and invited several more offers, including one that was reportedly made by U.S.-based Nucor (NUE), which might serve as a basis in the future should a buyer step in to pick up U.S. Steel (X) at a reduced price.

More on U.S. Steel

  • Steelworkers Union says Nippon Steel's last-ditch offer lacks commitment
  • United States Steel's Plunge Represents A Great Buying Opportunity
  • US Steel surges as Nippon Steel sends new proposal to Biden in final bid for support

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: X, CVNA, and more
data.description:
Stock futures remained cautious Friday morning following a volatile start to 2025, as investors awaited the latest ISM Manufacturing Index reading.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • SuperCom (SPCB) shares extended their rally in premarket trading on Friday, gaining an additional 10% after surging 59% in the previous session. The momentum follows the announcement of two new contracts with key agencies in Kentucky. These contracts involve deploying SuperCom's advanced public safety technologies, aimed at enhancing monitoring capabilities and delivering tailored, reliable solutions.

Biggest stock losers

  • Shares of United States Steel (X) plunged over 7% in amid reports that outgoing U.S. President Joe Biden plans to block Nippon Steel Corp.'s proposed $14.1B acquisition. This comes despite Nippon Steel’s offer to grant the U.S. government final authority over U.S. Steel’s production capacity to address White House concerns. While the president has signaled opposition, the White House has not formally confirmed the decision, though an announcement is expected as soon as Friday.
  • Carvana (CVNA) fell as much as 4% after Hindenburg Research published an unfavorable report, confirming it had taken a short position in the company based on its findings. In response, a Carvana spokesperson told Seeking Alpha that "the arguments in today's report are intentionally misleading and inaccurate" and have already been presented by other short sellers looking to benefit from a decline in the stock price. The spokesperson added that the company remains focused on executing its plan for a strong year in 2025.

data.title: Sandvik acquires CAM reseller FASTech
data.description:
• Sandvik (OTCPK:SDVKY) has acquired US-based FASTech, a reseller of Computer Aided Manufacturing (CAM) solutions in the Mastercam network, strengthening its position in the CAM market and expanding its customer base.
  • The purchase price was not disclosed.
  • FASTech will be part of Mastercam's business unit, and will be reported within business area Sandvik Manufacturing and Machining Solutions.
  • The impact on Sandvik's EBITA margin and earnings per share is expected to be limited.

data.title: Sandvik acquires computer-aided manufacturing reseller FASTech
data.description:
• Sandvik (OTCPK:SDVKY) Friday said that it has acquired the assets of FASTech, a U.S. based reseller of computer-aided manufacturing (CAM) solutions in the Mastercam network, expanding its customer base.
  • FASTech will be a part of business unit Mastercam and will be reported within business area Sandvik Manufacturing and Machining Solutions.
  • "This acquisition supports our strategic direction to grow in the digital manufacturing space, strengthens our regional presence, and enhances our ability to help our customers," Stefan Widing, President and CEO of Sandvik (OTCPK:SDVKY) said.
  • The impact on Sandvik's EBITA margin and earnings per share will be limited. The parties have agreed not to disclose the purchase price.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: European markets in red as global uncertainties weigh investor sentiment
data.description:
London (UKX) -0.10%

Germany (DAX:IND) -0.51% Germany's unemployment rate steady at 6.1% in December.

France (CAC:IND) -0.88%

Poland's annual inflation rate edged up to 4.8% in December.

Switzerland's procure.ch Manufacturing PMI edged down to 48.4 in December.

The Czech Republic's GDP grew by 0.5% Q/Q in 2024.

The unemployment rate in Hungary rose to 4.7% in September-November 2024.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) moved 0.41% lower, with mining stocks mostly negative, while financials led gains. Investors seem to be wary of global political uncertainties, including instability in France, German elections, and potential tariffs under US President-elect Donald Trump.

Coming up in the session: A lighter day on the economic front today.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield up 2 basis points to 4.55%.

Germany's 10-year yield up less than 1 basis point to 2.37%.

UK's 10-year yield was down 3 basis points to 4.56%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (EWG), (GF), (EWI), (EWQ), (FGM), (DAX), (FLGR), (FXB), (EWU), (FKU), (EWUS), (FLGB), (GREK)

data.title: Germany's unemployment rate steady at 6.1% in December
data.description:
Germany's seasonally adjusted unemployment rate remained at 6.1% in December 2024, slightly below market expectations of 6.2% but still near its highest level since February 2021.

The number of unemployed persons increased to 2869K in December from 2860K in November.

ETFs: (EWG), (GF), (FGM), (DAX), (FLGR) (DAX:IND)

Currency: (EUR:USD)

data.title: Tesla China sales hit record high level in December
data.description:
Though Tesla's (NASDAQ:TSLA) global deliveries slipped 1.1% in 2024, sales in China mustered up despite ongoing price wars and stiff competition from homegrown brands like BYD Company (OTCPK:BYDDF), NIO (NIO), and others.

Tesla (TSLA) car sales in China were up 12.8% month-over-month in December to a record high of 83,000 units, Reuters reported, citing Tesla China.

Overall in 2024, Tesla (TSLA) sold 657,000 units, up 8% year-over-year, and deliveries for the final quarter of 2024 were up 2.28% from 484,507. About 196,900 vehicles were sold in China in Q4, according to calculations CnEVPost.

The EV maker delivered 36.7% of its cars in the year 2024 to customers in China, its second-largest market, based on the sales figures.

Tesla (TSLA) extended discounts and zero-interest financing on some of its cars as the EV price war in China enters a third year. The U.S. EV giant (TSLA) also had to cut its sales team in China due to stiff competition and tepid demand.

data.title: Roadzen prices capital raise of $5M via stock offering
data.description:
• Roadzen (NASDAQ:RDZN) has announced the pricing of 2,222,300 ordinary shares at a public offering price of $2.25 per share, for gross proceeds of approximately $5 million.
  • All the ordinary shares are being offered by the company.
  • The offering is expected to close on January 6, 2025.
  • The company plans to use the net proceeds from the offering for sales, marketing, research, development, working capital, and general corporate purposes, including personnel and capital expenditures, and may also use a portion of the net proceeds to repay indebtedness outstanding.
